class_schedule.topic: Multiculturalism and the Literary Canon: Inclusion, Tokenism, and Transformation
learning_objectives: Analyze the debates over canon expansion and the politics of literary representation
Distinguish between additive multiculturalism (adding diverse texts to an unchanged canon) and transformative multiculturalism (rethinking what literature is and does)
Understand how identity categories intersect in multicultural literary analysis

lecture_notes.introduction: Multicultural literature is not a subcategory — it is a challenge to the category system itself. This course reads literature from marginalized communities not as supplementary to a white mainstream but as central to the American literary project.
key_concepts: Canon expansion vs. canon transformation: adding texts vs. changing what we value in literature
Identity and representation: who speaks, for whom, and with what authority
Borderlands thinking: Anzaldua's concept of living between cultures as a source of creative power
Intersectionality in literature: how texts address multiple, overlapping identities simultaneously
The politics of anthologization: how textbook selections shape which multicultural voices are heard
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the table of contents of three different American literature anthologies to reveal how editorial choices about multicultural inclusion shape students' understanding of literary history
setup_requirements: Three anthology tables of contents for comparison

review.value: Anzaldua's concept: the physical, linguistic, and psychological space of living between cultures — not a deficit but a rich, complex subject position that generates new forms of consciousness and expression
Going beyond adding diverse texts to an unchanged syllabus to fundamentally rethinking what counts as literature, what we value in it, and how we read it

class_schedule.topic: Native American Literature: Silko and the Power of Story
learning_objectives: Analyze how Native American literature draws on oral traditions, ceremonial structures, and communal storytelling
Understand Silko's concept of storytelling as a form of healing and cultural survival
Identify the ethical issues of reading Indigenous literature as an outsider
readings: Silko, 'Language and Literature from a Pueblo Indian Perspective' (essay)

lecture_notes.introduction: Native American literature challenges Western literary assumptions at every level: authorship (communal vs. individual), purpose (healing vs. entertainment), time (circular vs. linear), and the relationship between story and reality (stories are not representations of reality; they are reality).
key_concepts: Storytelling as ceremony: in Pueblo tradition, stories have the power to heal, to maintain the world, and to create reality
Oral tradition and literary form: how Silko incorporates oral storytelling patterns into written fiction
Ceremony's structure: Tayo's healing parallels the reader's experience of learning to read in a different way
The ethics of reading: how non-Native readers can engage with Indigenous literature without appropriating it
Survivance: Gerald Vizenor's term for the active practice of cultural survival and resistance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the opening of Ceremony — 'Ts'its'tsi'nako, Thought-Woman, / is sitting in her room / and whatever she thinks about / appears' — to discuss how the novel establishes storytelling as an act of creation, not just representation

review.value: In Silko's Pueblo tradition, stories are not entertainment or even art — they are ceremonial acts with real power to heal individuals and maintain the balance of the world; Ceremony enacts this principle as a novel

Vizenor's term combining 'survival' and 'resistance': the active, creative practice of maintaining Indigenous identity and culture through storytelling, art, and cultural production in the face of colonial erasure

class_schedule.topic: Asian American Literature: Identity, Assimilation, and Resistance
learning_objectives: Analyze how Asian American literature negotiates between cultural preservation and American assimilation
Identify the 'model minority' myth and its literary critique
Understand how Asian American writers use form to express the experience of cultural duality
readings: Kingston, The Woman Warrior, 'No Name Woman' and 'White Tigers'

lecture_notes.introduction: Asian American literature occupies a complex position: caught between the pressure to assimilate into a white mainstream and the desire to preserve cultural heritage, navigating stereotypes that cast Asian Americans as perpetual foreigners or model minorities.
key_concepts: The model minority myth: how it functions as a tool for dividing communities of color
Kingston's talk-story: blending Chinese myth with American experience to create a new form
The Woman Warrior as genre experiment: memoir, myth, fiction, and cultural critique fused together
The immigrant generation gap: parents' homeland memories vs. children's American present
Language and silence: how the inability or refusal to speak functions in Asian American writing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of 'No Name Woman,' analyzing how Kingston reconstructs her aunt's story from deliberate family silence — and how the act of telling the story is itself a feminist and cultural intervention

review.value: Kingston's adaptation of the Chinese oral tradition of talk-story — extravagant, digressive storytelling that blends fact, myth, and embellishment — as a literary form for rendering Chinese American experience in all its complexity

The stereotype of Asian Americans as universally successful, hardworking, and compliant, which functions to deny the reality of anti-Asian racism and to discipline other communities of color by comparison

class_schedule.topic: Latinx Literature: Borders, Languages, and Code-Switching
learning_objectives: Analyze how Latinx literature represents the experience of living between languages and cultures
Understand code-switching as a literary technique that enacts bilingual consciousness
Identify how border literature challenges the concept of a single national literature
readings: Cisneros, The House on Mango Street (complete)
Junot Diaz, 'How to Date a Browngirl, Blackgirl, Whitegirl, or Halfie'

lecture_notes.introduction: Latinx literature is written from the border — linguistic, cultural, national. Its writers move between English and Spanish, between the United States and Latin America, between assimilation and cultural preservation, and their literature reflects that movement in its very language and form.
key_concepts: Code-switching in literature: moving between English and Spanish within a single text as an enactment of bilingual experience
Cisneros's vignette form: short, lyrical sketches that refuse the conventional novel structure in favor of accumulation
The house as metaphor: Esperanza's desire for a house of her own echoes and revises Woolf's room of one's own
Diaz's second-person instruction: 'how to' as both parody and survival guide
The border as epistemology: knowing from the border means knowing multiplicity, contradiction, and translation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the untranslated Spanish in The House on Mango Street, discussing how Cisneros refuses to translate for an English-only reader and what that refusal means politically and aesthetically

review.value: The practice of moving between languages within a single text — in Latinx literature, code-switching is not a failure to choose a language but a formal representation of bilingual consciousness and bicultural identity

Cisneros's choice to build Mango Street from short, interconnected vignettes rather than a conventional plot mirrors the episodic, fragmented nature of childhood memory and the communal experience of a neighborhood

class_schedule.topic: LGBTQ+ Literature: Visibility, Voice, and the Queering of Form
learning_objectives: Analyze how LGBTQ+ literature challenges heteronormative assumptions in literary form and content
Understand queer theory's concept of normativity and its application to literary analysis
Identify how LGBTQ+ writers use formal experimentation to express experiences that conventional forms cannot contain

lecture_notes.introduction: LGBTQ+ literature does not merely add queer characters to existing forms. At its most powerful, it queers form itself — challenging the conventions of genre, narrative structure, and identity categories that organize literary tradition.
key_concepts: Queer as verb: queering means challenging normative categories, not just representing queer people
Baldwin's Giovanni's Room: an American in Paris, a novel about desire, shame, and the impossibility of belonging
Performativity: Butler's concept that identity is constituted through repeated performance, not expressed from an inner essence
Nelson's hybrid form: The Argonauts as memoir, theory, and love letter fused into a text that refuses genre categories
Coming-out narrative and its limits: when the standard story becomes its own constraint
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of the opening of Giovanni's Room, analyzing how Baldwin's prose style — elegant, tortured, circling around confession — formally enacts the narrator's struggle between desire and denial

review.value: Judith Butler's concept: gender and sexual identity are not inner essences expressed through behavior but are constituted through repeated performances of culturally available scripts — and those scripts can be disrupted, parodied, and rewritten

Using literary form to challenge normativity: Nelson's The Argonauts refuses to separate memoir from theory, love from philosophy, pregnancy from queer identity — the hybrid form IS the argument about the inadequacy of conventional categories

quizzes.topic: Multicultural Theory and Native American Literature

questions.question: What is the difference between additive and transformative multiculturalism in literary studies?
questions.answer: Additive multiculturalism simply adds diverse texts to an unchanged canon and curriculum — a week on Toni Morrison in a syllabus otherwise structured around white male authors. Transformative multiculturalism rethinks the fundamental categories: what counts as literature, what we value in it, how we organize literary history, and whose traditions define the standards of excellence.

questions.question: What does Silko mean when she says that in Pueblo tradition, stories are not representations of reality but are reality? How does this affect how we should read Ceremony?
questions.answer: For Silko, stories have actual power — they create and maintain the world, heal individuals, and sustain the community's relationship with the land. They are not symbolic representations of something else; they are ceremonial acts with real effects. This means Ceremony should be read not just as a novel about healing but as a healing act itself — the structure of the novel parallels the structure of a ceremony, and reading it is meant to be a transformative experience, not just an aesthetic one.

rubric.key: Explains stories as having real power, not just symbolic meaning
Connects to Ceremony as a healing act
Notes the implications for how we read the novel

questions.question: Why does Cisneros leave Spanish untranslated in The House on Mango Street? What is the political and aesthetic significance of this choice?
questions.answer: Cisneros refuses to translate Spanish for English-only readers as a political act: it insists that bilingual experience is the text's reality, not a deviation from an English norm. Aesthetically, the untranslated Spanish creates moments where monolingual readers must either learn, guess from context, or accept incomprehension — placing them in the position of linguistic outsider that bilingual people navigate constantly.

rubric.key: Identifies the political dimension of refusing to translate
Explains the aesthetic effect on monolingual readers
Connects to the bilingual experience the text represents

questions.question: What does Judith Butler mean by 'performativity' in relation to gender, and how does this concept apply to LGBTQ+ literature?
questions.answer: Butler argues that gender is not an inner essence expressed through behavior but is constituted through the repeated performance of culturally available scripts — there is no gender identity behind the performance; the performance IS the identity. For LGBTQ+ literature, this means that queer writing can challenge normative identity categories not just by representing queer people but by disrupting the narrative forms and genre conventions through which normative identities are constructed and reinforced.

rubric.key: Distinguishes from essentialist views of identity
Connects to literary form, not just content

major_assignments.title: Multicultural Literature Analysis
major_assignments.description: Write a 2,500-3,000 word essay analyzing how one or two multicultural texts engage with questions of identity, representation, or belonging. The essay must use an appropriate critical framework (postcolonial, ethnic studies, queer theory, etc.) and engage with at least three scholarly sources.
objectives: Analyze multicultural literature with cultural specificity and critical sophistication
Apply an appropriate theoretical framework without reductive generalization
Demonstrate awareness of your own reading position and its limitations
Integrate scholarly perspectives from the relevant critical tradition
deliverables: Works Cited with primary text(s) and at least 3 scholarly sources
500-word positionality statement reflecting on your reading position

criteria: Analysis demonstrates cultural specificity, not generalizations about 'diverse' experience
Theoretical framework is appropriate to the text's cultural context
Student avoids both tokenism and universalizing
Textual evidence is specific and carefully analyzed
Form and style are connected to cultural meaning
Analysis reveals something that a culturally unaware reading would miss
Both texts receive substantive analysis if two are chosen
Prose is clear, precise, and well-organized
Positionality statement is honest and reflective
Culturally specific analysis avoiding tokenism and generalization
Detailed close reading connecting form to cultural meaning
Effective use of appropriate critical framework
Clear academic prose with honest positionality reflection

analysis_essay.description: Multicultural literature analysis with theoretical framework

reading_responses.description: Weekly responses engaging with assigned multicultural texts

quizzes.description: Quizzes on critical concepts and assigned readings

seminar_participation.description: Quality and respectfulness of seminar discussion

final_project.description: Final project: researched presentation on a multicultural literary tradition not covered in the syllabus

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory. More than 2 unexcused absences result in a letter grade deduction.
course_policies.collaboration: All written work is individual. Seminar discussion requires respect for diverse perspectives and willingness to engage with unfamiliar cultural contexts.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism results in a zero and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Assigned novels and critical texts, word processing software, library database access, and LMS access.
